title: Server Configuration, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, TLS, and Graceful Shutdown · Guided Systems Studio
summary: A server pairing session predicts request, handler, dependency, response, and cleanup transitions before building the first explicit http.Server protocol and shutdown configuration for server configuration, http/1.1, http/2, tls, and graceful shutdown. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of explicit http.Server protocol and shutdown configuration while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debug…
objectives: Configure ReadHeaderTimeout, body strategy, WriteTimeout, IdleTimeout, MaxHeaderBytes, and per-handler deadlines from threat and workload evidence.
Configure Server.Protocols for HTTP/1 and TLS HTTP/2, treat UnencryptedHTTP2 as explicit h2c authority, and name HTTP/3 as outside net/http server support.
Set HTTP2Config stream, frame, compression-table, flow-control, ping, write-byte, and error-count boundaries without confusing transport and server fields.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Server Timeout Header Limits
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop team handles case 8600 in a civic incident API behind one trusted regional proxy. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 service by timeout and graceful-drain policy while excluding HTTP/3; then lower the byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs at the owning boundary. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Configure ReadHeaderTimeout, body strategy, WriteTimeout, IdleTimeout, MaxHeaderBytes, and per-handler deadlines from threat and workload evidence.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Server Timeout Header Limits.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for configure readheadertimeout, body strategy, writetimeout, idletimeout, maxheaderbytes, and per-handler deadlines from threat and workload evidence. For Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Server Timeout Header Limits, keep Server Timeout Header Limits connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

hints: For Go server Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Shutdown Close Register, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Shutdown Close Register precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Go server Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Shutdown Close Register, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop team handles case 8608 in a privacy-sensitive records API using conditional requests. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 service by timeout and graceful-drain policy while excluding HTTP/3; then remove one trusted intermediary and recompute authority without forwarded fields. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use Shutdown deadlines, readiness withdrawal, listener stop, in-flight drain, hijacked-connection handling, RegisterOnShutdown, Close fallback, and exit evidence.

For Go server Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Shutdown Close Register, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Server.Shutdown waits for hijacked connections and guarantees every cleanup callback has finished.
